name: B.S. in Chemical Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The eight parsed semesters (requirementGroups 8) follow the canonical chemical-engineering ladder with exemplary ordering: foundation (CHEM 125/126, MATH 111/112, PHYS 111/121, CS 115, CHE 101 intro) into the core unit-operations spine (CHE 201 Material and Energy Balances, CHE 230/342 Thermodynamics I/II, CHE 260 Fluid Flow, CHE 370 Heat and Mass Transfer, CHE 349 Kinetics and Reactor Design, CHE 360 Separation Processes) and culminating in CHE 489 Process Dynamics and Control, CHE 495/496 laboratories and CHE 472 Process and Plant Design. Supporting chemistry (CHEM 236 P-Chem, CHEM 243/244A Organic) and CHE 312 Process Safety are placed exactly where the discipline expects them.
dimensions.suggestion: Surface a one-line 'core spine' annotation (balances to thermo to transport to kinetics to separations to design) so prospective students can see the signature ChemE progression at a glance.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: This is the deepest and most tightly sequenced graph in the college (deepestPrereqChain 10), which is appropriate rather than excessive for ChemE's linear core: CHE 472 Plant Design correctly gates on CHE 312, CHE 349, CHE 360, CHE 365 and IE 492, and prerequisites use clear grade conditions and co-requisites (CHE 365 'CHE 370, CS 115, co-requisite CHE 360'). missingCourseCount is 7, but the missingCoursesSample (CHE 101, CHEM 125A, ENGR 211, GER 200, MATH 225, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A) is dominated by 1-credit intro/lab sections and GER placeholders, not broken technical links.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the seven dangling references, especially the low-credit core touchpoints CHE 101, ENGR 211 and MATH 225, so the deep core chain has no unmatched nodes; and verify the depth-10 path is achievable without scheduling bottlenecks.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives are present in the data; only the plan of study and course descriptions are captured. Given the strongly ABET-aligned structure (a dedicated capstone design course, process safety, and engineering ethics), the absence of published outcomes is a genuine catalog gap rather than a reflection of the curriculum.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit outcomes covering process design under safety/economic constraints, mass/energy/momentum transport analysis, and reactor/separation design, mapped to the CHE 472 capstone.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (summedTermCredits 120, statedTotalCredits 120, creditReconcileDelta 0) and is well balanced between basic science, the ChemE core, laboratory work (CHEM 339, CHE 495/496) and required professional/ethics content (IE 492, PHIL 334 Engineering Ethics, COM 313 Technical Writing). The one balance limitation is elective breadth: only two Technical Elective slots exist, leaving little room for students to specialize.
dimensions.suggestion: Expand from two to three or four technical-elective credits (or add a designated concentration track) so students can build depth in an area such as bioprocessing, energy or materials.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The catalog page is complete and highly legible: eight clearly labeled terms with per-term credit subtotals, a full course title for every item, and inclusion of professional-formation content (PHIL 334 Engineering Ethics, COM 313 Technical Writing) that many plans omit. Only a couple of GER rows carry null credit values, a very minor blemish on an otherwise clean layout.
dimensions.suggestion: Assign explicit credit values to the two GER placeholder rows (History/Humanities and the HSS Senior Seminar) so all eight terms sum visibly to 120.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum is strongly current for core process engineering: CHE 312 Chemical Process Safety, CHE 365 Chemical Engineering Computing, CHE 489 Process Dynamics and Control and CHE 472 Plant Design map directly onto practicing-engineer competencies, and MTEN 201 adds materials grounding. The main currency gaps are the absence of sustainability/decarbonization content, data-driven / machine-learning process methods, and any bioprocess-engineering exposure despite the department's biochemistry strength.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a sustainability/process-intensification or bioprocess elective and modernize CHE 365 and CHE 489 to include data-driven modeling and digital-twin/process-analytics techniques.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 33 sampled courses have present, specific descriptions with none thin: e.g., CHE 201 detailing material/energy balances and unit operations, CHE 312 covering plant-accident impact and individual/societal risk, CHE 365 on numerical methods for process simulation, and CHE 472 framing the capstone around engineering standards and public-health/safety constraints. Only excerpt length is truncated.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are clearly and consistently expressed, including co-requisites (CHE 365 'co-requisite: CHE 360') and multi-course design gates (CHE 472 lists six prerequisites), and grade conditions are stated on the science chain. The deduction is for seven dangling references (CHE 101, CHEM 125A, ENGR 211, GER 200, MATH 225, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A) that do not resolve in the catalog.

missingTopics.topic: Sustainability, decarbonization and green process design
missingTopics.rationale: CHE 472 Plant Design and CHE 360 Separations are the natural place to embed carbon capture, electrification and green-solvent selection, yet the plan has no sustainability course and only two technical electives.

missingTopics.topic: Data-driven process modeling and machine learning
missingTopics.rationale: CHE 365 Chemical Engineering Computing and CHE 489 Process Dynamics and Control already teach simulation and control, but neither covers data-driven/ML surrogate models now standard in process optimization.
The department offers biochemistry (CHEM 473 appears in the broader pool) yet the ChemE plan has no fermentation/bioreactor or downstream-bioprocessing course despite growing biomanufacturing demand.
With CHE 349 Kinetics and CHE 342 Thermodynamics II in place, an electrochemistry/battery elective would connect existing fundamentals to the energy-transition job market.
The plan emphasizes bulk process/plant design (CHE 472) but not consumer-product/formulated-product design, an area of growing ChemE employment in specialty chemicals and pharma.

emergingTrends.trend: Decarbonization, carbon capture and the hydrogen economy
emergingTrends.rationale: CHE 360 separations and CHE 472 design provide the ideal vehicles to teach CO2 capture and hydrogen/electrofuel process design, aligning the program with the energy transition.

emergingTrends.trend: Digital twins and data-driven process operations
emergingTrends.rationale: CHE 489 control and CHE 365 computing can be modernized toward real-time process analytics and digital-twin operation, a fast-growing plant-engineering practice.
Building on the department's organic/biochemistry strength, bioreactor and cell-culture process content positions graduates for the expanding biopharma sector.
Life-cycle thinking and process intensification woven into CHE 472 would reflect the shift toward circular-economy chemical production.
Coupling CHE 349 kinetics with electrochemistry supports the surge in battery, fuel-cell and electrochemical-manufacturing roles.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes / educational objectives (currently absent), mapped to the CHE 472 capstone and CHE 312 process-safety competencies.
Add a sustainability/decarbonization or bioprocess elective to close the program's biggest currency gap and reflect where ChemE hiring is moving.
Modernize CHE 365 (Computing) and CHE 489 (Control) to include data-driven/ML process modeling and digital-twin techniques.
Expand technical-elective capacity beyond the current two slots (or add a named concentration) so students can specialize in energy, materials or bioprocessing.
Resolve the seven dangling references (notably CHE 101, ENGR 211, MATH 225) so the deep depth-10 core chain is fully connected in the catalog.
